Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Reuter handles content for "Viewpoints," a public affairs program. "My beat is any topic that's not health-related," she says. "I do stories on politics, pop culture, the economy, education, the poor, employment, public safety, infrastructure, arts & entertainment, science and technology, agriculture, space, crime, history and law." She doesn't want the traditional "self-help" pitches on relationship, diets and exercise or new age cures. "I have done stories on young people and how their dating behavior has changed," she says. "I also like fun topics. I just finished a story on why we're fascinated by magic. I've done stories on famous people and famous battles from various wars." The best PR people get good sources, not self-published authors or "self-proclaimed" experts, send her an advanced book copy before the interview, get back to her with interview details such as time and phone number and can find a quality source when she calls about a story she's working on. Contact by email.

Contact Profile: Prefers to be contacted by Mail, Fax, Phone No diets or self help. Very issue-oriented. Four week lead time needed. <From Bulldog> "We tend to follow new developments and new technologies" related to healthcare and medicine, says host/producer Mr. Reed Pence. "If it's breaking news, we will definitely cover it." He shies away from self-help and "news you can use" consumer-health coverage. "What we're really trying to do is give perspective about what new laws and policies and research findings mean to them," he says. He appreciates five to six weeks' notice of forthcoming research news. He looks for experts who have "good academic credentials, and are well respected and generally recognized in their fields." He'll consider pharma and medical device news. "When they have some very promising results from perhaps a Phase 2 or later clinical trial, that's when you really start to look at things," he says. Help him cut through the clutter: "PR people need to realize that there is a huge amount of health and medical information out there right now," Pence advises. "There's a real process we have to go through in winnowing out what's going to make it on the air and what's not. If we can get a heads-up that quickly cuts to the chase --- what it means for people who will be listening to this program --- that's really what it's all about." Pence prefers E-mail. "Phone calls are OK, but E-mail lets me sort through it a little more easily," he says.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> "I am the Senior Producer of `Hello Beautiful,' an arts program that airs Sunday mornings at 10 a.m. CST," Cunningham says. "We feature local Chicago artists such as performing, visual, literature, anything that has to do with the arts," he says. He also does a segment called "Three to See" a weekly events calendar that covers the same kind of arts. If there's a connection to or about Chicago arts, he'll cover nationally. "If a touring theater is coming, I don't just do it to plug a performance, it has to have relevance," he says. "One photographer was not from Chicago, he was from N.Y. but had spent time with an author from here who had passed away, so he had a connection," he says. Give him two to four weeks lead time. Make emails "text with attachments, no more than a page with pictures already on it," he says.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Moller hosts a three-hour Saturday afternoon talk show. "I look for guests to talk about personal finance, consumer and small business issues, and take questions from callers. The audience is sophisticated people in their 30's and up. I want guests to offer the listeners practical things like how to manage money or get out of debt. I'm not interested in people talking about how to be a better CEO or make a million dollars in two weeks," he says. "I like to have guests in studio, but they can be on the phone. Pitch one to two weeks in advance. Keep it concise, tell me in a few words what's compelling," he advises.
